system_description.overview: Toffu AI is an AI-powered marketing automation platform designed to help B2B SaaS companies grow. The platform acts as an autonomous marketing agent that automates tasks including blog creation, social media content, email campaigns, and performance tracking, generating human-quality content and driving lead generation with minimal supervision.

products.AI-Powered Marketing Automation: Automates marketing workflows including blog generation, social media content, and email campaigns with performance tracking
products.Content Generation and Multi-Channel Pub: AI-generated, human-quality content distributed automatically across blogs, social media, and email
products.Lead Generation and Conversion Optimizat: Inbound lead generation tools with analytics integrations for measuring campaign effectiveness
products.Custom Playbooks and Workflow Automation: Structured marketing playbooks with flexible automation logic for complex marketing tasks
products.Customer Support and Onboarding: White-glove onboarding, implementation support, and ongoing access to customer success specialists

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — physical security delegated to AWS as subservice organization)
regions: Not explicitly named — single region implied from architecture
infrastructure.physical_presence: 651 N Broad St, Middletown, DE 19709 (no physical servers; all data on cloud)
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — 'continuity of operations in the event of an availability zone failure by spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS'

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with ACLs (access control lists) to control traffic between network segments; system components configured so company and customer access is segmented from other customer accounts (CC6.1.3)
network_architecture.api_gateway: API Gateway — explicitly shown in network diagram as entry point from API clients alongside Web Interface
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services; firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management (CC6.6.1)
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via AWS — employs IDS and IPS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings; formal network diagram maintained with boundary protection including IDS (CC2.3.4)
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN — 'Remote access is done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN)'
network_architecture.tls: TLS with certificates from established third-party CA; TLS encryption (OAuth 2.0 keys) explicitly shown in network diagram for social media API connections
network_architecture.content_filtering: Proxy server — all systems restricted to internet through content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Web Users + API Clients + Slack/WhatsApp → Web Interface / API Gateway / Platform Handlers → Authorization (JWT tokens, Role-Based Access) → Rate Limiting/Track Limiter → Supervisor Manager → [Task Classifier / MongoDB (Primary Database) / Redis (Session/Stream Data) / Image Events / Sentry (Error Tracking) / Usage Recording / Audit Logging] → [Claude 3.7 / GPT-4 / GPT-4 Mini AI models] → [Google Workspace / Social Media APIs (LinkedIn/Twitter/Meta) / Marketing Tools (Google Analytics) / Communication (Email/Slack) / Vector DB / Credit Management] → Real-time WebSocket to Web Interface; File Uploads → File Processing → Temporary Storage → Google Drive / Content Generation (HTML/Markdown)

application_architecture.pattern: Microservices with Supervisor Manager orchestration — Task Classifier routes workflows to AI models (Claude 3.7, GPT-4, GPT-4 Mini), real-time streaming via Redis Streams and WebSocket
compute: AWS EC2 or equivalent (implied — servers in AWS VPC)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments; changes tested in separate dev/test environment before production deployment (CC8.1.4)

databases: MongoDB (explicitly shown as 'Primary Database' in network diagram — user data encrypted, company data encrypted, integration tokens encrypted)
Redis (explicitly shown — session/stream data, real-time processing via Redis Streams)
caching: Redis (explicitly shown for session caching and real-time stream processing)
file_storage: Temporary Storage for file processing (shown in diagram)
Google Drive (document storage/generation — explicitly shown in diagram)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Backup policy established; formal backup and recovery procedures; frequency not explicitly stated
data_storage.per_tenant_segregation: Customer access segmented from other customer accounts (CC6.1.3) — multi-tenancy isolation confirmed

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: JWT tokens with Role-Based Access (explicitly shown in network diagram — 'Authorization: JWT Tokens, Role-Based Access' layer); Active Directory for server accounts; password policy via AWS
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); 2FA required for employee email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within 1 business day of termination — 'access is revoked for terminated employees within one business day' (CC6.1.4)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Via AWS password policy; periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements; account disabled after limited failed logon attempts; no guest/anonymous logins; unattended desktop lockout with biometrics to unlock

encryption.in_transit: TLS with certificates from established third-party CA; TLS encryption (OAuth 2.0 keys) for data in transit to social media APIs explicitly noted in diagram
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (AWS KMS) — 'encryption at rest: AES-128-CSE' explicitly shown in network diagram (Image Events node); data in MongoDB encrypted at field level (User Data Encrypted, Company Data Encrypted, Integration Tokens Encrypted)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS — 'cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest and to store and manage encryption keys'; 'TLS Encryption, OAuth/API Keys' shown in network diagram for external API connections

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Git-based SCM tool — 'source code management tool' referenced; code repository branch rules configured (CC8.1.2); access restricted via MFA (CC8.1.3)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to production requires additional approval; developers cannot make production code changes without approval (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configuration implementation and rollback capability (CC7.1.1) — 'baseline configurations are retained within the infrastructure as a code'; Sentry shown in diagram for error tracking
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO/Management sets up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates; workstations scanned for patch compliance daily; patches tested for stability/availability before production deployment

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Sentry (explicitly shown in network diagram as 'Sentry: Error Tracking, Token/Cost Tracking'); monitoring tool with predefined thresholds; performance monitoring covers disc space, incoming/outgoing network traffic, packet loss, CPU utilization
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1); 'Audit Logging: Activity Monitoring' and 'Log Retention: 90+ Days' explicitly shown in network diagram; access to log configuration and modification is restricted
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; issues resolved via incident management process; 'Usage Recording: Token/Cost Tracking' and 'Cost Analysis: Usage Limits' shown in diagram

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure (IaaS) — VPC, EC2, KMS; subservice organization under carve-out method
AI models for marketing content generation — explicitly shown in network diagram
AI model for complex task completion — explicitly shown in network diagram alongside GPT-4
Primary database for user data, company data, and integration tokens — explicitly named in network diagram
Caching and real-time stream processing (Redis Streams) — explicitly named in network diagram
Error tracking and token/cost monitoring — explicitly named in network diagram
Document storage and content generation output — explicitly shown in network diagram
Multi-channel content publishing — explicitly shown in network diagram
Marketing performance analytics — explicitly shown in network diagram as 'Marketing Tools: Google Ads/Analytics'
Campaign delivery and notifications — explicitly shown in network diagram

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection installed and configured on all production servers (continuous auto-scan; definitions updated weekly); also on Mac workstations/laptops; endpoint security solution with log review by IT Head
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; scan frequency adjusted as required; high/critical vulnerabilities remediated per incident response plan
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration test by independent third party; high/critical findings remediated and tracked to resolution (CC4.1.1)

bcdr.daily_backups: Backup policy established but frequency not explicitly stated as daily

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical facility access controls excluded (AWS responsibility as subservice org; no physical servers)
A1.2 — Environmental protections excluded (Toffu AI operates from coworking/leased space; building management and AWS responsibility)
Processing Integrity — determined not relevant to the system
Privacy — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.3 — Source code tool access restricted to authorized users via MFA
CC6.1.3 — System components configured so company and customer access is segmented from other customer accounts (multi-tenancy isolation)
CC6.1.4 — 1-business-day access revocation SLA explicitly stated
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all user accounts including admin-privileged
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration test; high/critical findings tracked to resolution
CC7.1.1 — Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for baseline configuration with rollback capability
CC9.2.1 — Quarterly vendor risk assessments for critical vendors lacking SOC reports
CC9.2.2 — Vendor risk assessments performed prior to onboarding new vendors
CC9.2.3 — Vendor termination procedures including access revocation checklist
CC7.4.1 — BCDR tested annually against multiple defined loss scenarios; plans updated based on test results
CC2.1.4 — CISO role assigned; defined roles and responsibilities for security oversight including incident response

infrastructure.rationale: AWS VPC with segmentation, multi-AZ, bastion host VPN, IDS/IPS, proxy server content filtering. Single region. No CDN, no WAF despite API Gateway and multi-channel publishing. No secondary cloud. Standard AWS single-region setup.

application_architecture.rationale: Best application architecture disclosure in the entire batch. Network diagram on page 18 explicitly names: MongoDB (primary DB), Redis (cache + streams), Claude 3.7, GPT-4, GPT-4 Mini, Sentry (error tracking), Redis Streams (real-time), WebSocket, JWT auth, Role-Based Access, Google Drive, LinkedIn/Twitter/Meta APIs, Google Analytics, Email/Slack APIs, TLS+OAuth keys, AES-128-CSE encryption at rest. Microservices with Supervisor Manager orchestration. Missing only: language/framework names and explicit compute type.

data_layer.rationale: MongoDB as primary database explicitly named. Redis for caching and real-time streams explicitly named. Field-level encryption (User Data Encrypted, Company Data Encrypted, Integration Tokens Encrypted) shown in diagram. AES-128-CSE for data at rest. Log retention 90+ days explicitly shown. Backup policy exists but daily frequency not confirmed. No data warehouse mentioned.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly automated vuln scans, IDS/IPS via AWS, anti-virus on production servers (weekly definitions), endpoint protection on workstations, MFA on admin access and source code, firewall with annual review, content filtering via proxy, quarterly access reviews, 1-day revocation SLA, CISO assigned, Sentry for error/anomaly tracking. No WAF despite API Gateway. No cybersecurity insurance. Field-level data encryption is a positive.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC confirmed with rollback, branch protection with MFA for SCM, emergency change process, separate environments, monthly patch reviews with automatic patch management, daily workstation compliance scanning, Sentry for production monitoring. CI/CD tool not named. No container orchestration mentioned. The AI model orchestration (Supervisor Manager + Task Classifier) suggests sophisticated deployment pipeline not visible in report.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists and is tested annually against multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover in AWS. DR plan with BIA, roles, critical systems, time frames. No multi-region failover. No RTO/RPO disclosed. Backup frequency not stated. Single cloud provider limits DR options.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Most extensive named vendor list in the batch by far: OpenAI (GPT-4 + GPT-4 Mini), Anthropic (Claude 3.7), MongoDB, Redis, Sentry, Google Workspace/Drive, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Meta, Google Analytics, Email APIs, Slack — 10+ named vendors beyond AWS. Multi-LLM architecture (two AI providers) reduces single-model dependency. Quarterly vendor risk assessments and pre-onboarding assessments. Concentration risk: simultaneous dependency on two LLM providers for core product function.

overall.rationale: Toffu AI has the most detailed technology disclosure of any company in this batch by a significant margin. The network diagram on page 18 is extraordinarily detailed, revealing MongoDB, Redis, Claude 3.7, GPT-4, GPT-4 Mini, Sentry, Redis Streams, WebSocket, TLS+OAuth key handling, AES-128-CSE encryption, Google Drive, and 5+ social/marketing API integrations. Management assertion is signed (Or Arbel, CEO, Jul 20, 2025). SOC 2 Type 1 limits assurance to design only. The dual-LLM architecture (Anthropic + OpenAI) and the breadth of external API integrations create unique security considerations not addressed by standard SOC 2 Security criteria.

red_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only — point-in-time design assessment; no operating effectiveness testing

Dual-LLM dependency (Anthropic Claude 3.7 + OpenAI GPT-4) with no AI-specific security controls in scope — no controls for prompt injection, data poisoning, model output validation, or LLM API key management beyond standard secret rotation
No WAF despite API Gateway serving B2B SaaS customers and 5+ external social/marketing API integrations
Single AWS region — no multi-region failover for a platform that manages live marketing campaigns across LinkedIn, Twitter, Meta, email, and Google
yellow_flags: No RTO or RPO disclosed despite managing live marketing campaigns where availability directly impacts customer revenue
No CI/CD pipeline named — deployment process for production AI models and microservices unspecified
Backup frequency not stated — critical for MongoDB primary database and Redis stream data
No CDN mentioned despite serving web content globally
Processing Integrity and Privacy excluded from scope despite platform generating and publishing content on behalf of customers to external social media platforms
MongoDB + Redis + Google Drive + 10+ external API vendor concentration not independently audited
green_flags: Most detailed network diagram in the entire batch — explicitly names MongoDB, Redis, Claude 3.7, GPT-4, GPT-4 Mini, Sentry, Redis Streams, WebSocket, JWT auth, AES-128-CSE encryption, Google Drive, LinkedIn/Twitter/Meta/Google Analytics/Email/Slack APIs
Field-level encryption in MongoDB — 'User Data Encrypted, Company Data Encrypted, Integration Tokens Encrypted' explicitly shown in diagram
AES-128-CSE encryption at rest explicitly named (uncommon level of encryption specificity in SOC 2 reports)
Dual-LLM architecture (Anthropic Claude 3.7 + OpenAI GPT-4) — reduces single-provider AI dependency
Sentry for error tracking and token/cost monitoring explicitly shown — operational observability
Log retention 90+ days explicitly shown in network diagram
Management assertion actually signed (Or Arbel, CEO, Jul 20, 2025)
Annual external penetration test by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scans with remediation tracking
MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3)
IaC with rollback capability for all baseline configurations
Quarterly user access reviews including admin-privileged accounts
Quarterly vendor risk assessments (CC9.2.1) + pre-onboarding vendor assessments (CC9.2.2)
CISO role assigned with defined responsibilities for security oversight
Content filtering via proxy server for all internet access
BCDR tested annually against multiple defined loss scenarios
key_observations: The network diagram on page 18 is by far the most technically detailed in this batch — it reveals a sophisticated multi-layer architecture: API Gateway + Web Interface → JWT/RBAC auth → Rate Limiting → Supervisor Manager orchestration → Task Classifier (routes by complexity) → AI models (Claude 3.7 for complex, GPT-4 for simple, GPT-4 Mini for trivial) → Redis Streams for real-time → WebSocket to UI. This level of architectural transparency is exceptional for a SOC 2 report.

The dual-LLM architecture (Anthropic Claude 3.7 + OpenAI GPT-4 + GPT-4 Mini) with a Task Classifier routing by complexity is architecturally sophisticated — using Claude for complex tasks and GPT-4 Mini for trivial tasks suggests deliberate cost/capability optimization. This also creates a unique attack surface: prompt injection attacks could potentially be routed to more capable models

AES-128-CSE (Client-Side Encryption with AES-128) explicitly shown for image events data at rest is notable — most SOC 2 reports only reference 'encryption at rest' without specifying algorithm. However, AES-128 is weaker than the industry-standard AES-256; this may be a concern for high-sensitivity customer data

MongoDB with field-level encryption for integration tokens is a strong control — OAuth tokens for LinkedIn, Twitter, Meta, Google, Slack, and email are stored encrypted at the field level, not just at the database level. This limits blast radius if the database is compromised

The platform publishes content to LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Meta, blogs, and email on behalf of customers — this creates significant reputational and legal risk if the platform is compromised, as an attacker could publish malicious content to all customer social media accounts simultaneously. This attack surface is not addressed by the Security-only trust criteria

No Privacy or Processing Integrity criteria despite being an autonomous marketing agent that creates and publishes content on behalf of customers to external platforms — the platform's AI-generated outputs directly represent customer brands to the public, creating significant Processing Integrity risk not covered by the SOC 2 scope
